story:
“Don’t you know it’s rude to turn up in a woman’s bedroom uninvited?” I said to the six visitors whose feelings were hard to read on their glistening, shifting tentacles. One balanced on the metal bed rail behind me; none dared come closer—until a cool, damp limb brushed my collarbone. Fear rose—and dissolved as music filled my mind, not sound but meaning rendered as a symphony.

“We require your skin for communication,” the message sang, its beauty almost distracting me from how strange this was.

“I understand,” I answered, pulled between awe and etiquette. “But next time, knock.”

“Your sensory modalities are new to us,” came the reply, a gentle chord. “We must investigate them.”

They approached and rested limbs on my ankles, forearms, the back of my neck. Each new touch was like adding an instrument to an unfolding orchestra. With every chord, fear drifted further away.

“This species communicates over distance using pressure waves,” one of their songs observed with delighted wonder.

“You can’t?” I asked.

“It is not our way. Our voices are as you experience them now.”

“Your world must be so different.”

“In ways you cannot begin to understand,” they sang, and the melody turned a little sad.

“Teach me,” I said. “And please—learn to knock.”

After that, they did: a respectful thumping on the door or window, a few times each week, and the orchestra arrived. With their tentacular touch into my mind, I showed them the felt textures of being human: the warmth and purr of a cat under the palm; the blaze of beef rendang on the tongue; the slow glide over Sentosa Island from the HarbourFront cable car; the ripple of music that moves air, not just thought. They shared their planet in turn: maze-like spires riddled with tunnels, a world navigated almost entirely by touch, where meaning resonated skin to skin. Each exchange was a harmony, and going back to life outside—traffic on Raffles Place, managers with memos—grew more discordant with every visit. Nothing rivaled their music.

Each had a name among their own kind—ancient, untranslatable—so I offered mine: Sonata, Pantoum, Erhu, Contralto, Fugue, and for my favorite, Rachmaninoff. Explaining why took an evening of patient melody and memory; I think they understood.

On the thirty-fifth day, Rachmaninoff’s voice arrived in a minor key: “Our time is concluded. We must return to Orchestra Prime.” The others felt my sadness like a sustained note. Then: “We are authorized to take you with us, if you desire.”

I looked around my room, at the life whose colors had faded beside their symphonies. The world I knew had grown quiet, hollowed by comparison to the resonant fullness they brought. I hesitated only as long as it took my heart to recognize what it wanted.

“I do.”

“Then let us travel,” Rachmaninoff sang, the chord widening until it felt like a doorway. Their limbs found my skin, and the music rose—not sound, but a boundless, wordless yes—until the room, the city, the weight of ordinary days all fell away, and I went with them willingly into the vast, singing dark.
